Marine biologists recently documented a pod of orcas hunting great white sharks off the coast of South Africa. The whales targeted the liver specifically, flipping the sharks to induce tonic immobility before consuming the nutrient-rich organ.

The behavior appears to be learned and transmitted between pods. Researchers identified distinct hunting dialects using underwater hydrophones, suggesting cultural transmission of predatory techniques among marine mammal populations.

Conservationists are concerned that the decline in shark populations may destabilize kelp forest ecosystems. Without apex predators to control mesopredator abundance, urchin barrens could expand along the coastline and destroy biodiversity hotspots.
